questions.prompt: What makes arrow functions different from regular functions regarding this?
options: Arrow functions always bind this to window
Arrow functions do not have their own this — they inherit it lexically
Arrow functions use strict mode this binding only
Arrow functions bind this dynamically
questions.explanation: Arrow functions capture this from the enclosing lexical scope at creation time. They have no own this.

questions.prompt: A function declaration called BEFORE its line in the file — what happens?
options: ReferenceError
undefined is returned
Works fine — function declarations are fully hoisted
TypeError
questions.explanation: Function declarations are fully hoisted, including the function body. They are callable anywhere in their scope.

options: Scope is determined at runtime based on the call stack
Scope is determined by where code is written in the source file
Each function creates a new global scope
Variables are visible everywhere in the module
questions.explanation: Lexical (static) scope is determined at author time — where the code is written, not where it's called.

options: A private class method
A function that retains access to its outer scope after that scope has exited
A sealed object
A module pattern
questions.explanation: A closure is a function plus its retained reference to the surrounding lexical environment.

questions.prompt: Write a createMultiplier(factor) factory function that uses a closure.
questions.answer_key:
function createMultiplier(factor) { return (n) => n * factor; }
const double = createMultiplier(2); double(5); // 10

questions.prompt: Can an outer function access a variable declared inside an inner function?
options: Yes — all variables are shared in closures
No — the scope chain is one-directional: inner accesses outer, not vice versa
Only if the inner function returns the variable
Yes, but only in non-strict mode
questions.explanation: Scope chains flow outward only. The outer function cannot see the inner function's variables.

questions.prompt: What TypeScript syntax types a function that takes (id: number, name: string) and returns void?
options: type F = (id: number, name: string) => void
type F = function(number, string): void
interface F { (id: number, name: string): void }
Both A and C
questions.explanation: Both type aliases and call-signature interfaces can describe function types in TypeScript.
